title: Game Modes
seoTitle: Game of Darts Game Modes | BarkinMad Studios
description: Game of Darts includes competitive match formats and focused training modes so players can choose the best format for match play, skill practice, or confidence building.
summary: Compare Match/X01, Cricket, Snooker, Bob's 27, One to 20, Hare & Hound, Halve It, and All 5s, then choose the best mode for your goals.
heroImageAlt: Artwork for Game of Darts game modes

sections.heading: Game formats for both matches and practice
paragraphs: Game of Darts is built around two usage paths: traditional match formats and focused training modes.
Together, these formats let you work on high-pressure scoring and finishing, while also helping you play complete match-style sessions with friends or DartBot.
bullets: Match-oriented play: X01, Cricket, and Snooker Darts.
Practice-oriented play: Bob's 27, One to 20, Hare & Hound, Halve It, and All 5s.

sections.heading: Choosing a game mode
paragraphs: Use this page to match mode selection to what you want from your session.
bullets: X01 is ideal for standard match play because it matches the format used in most match settings.
Cricket offers tactical gameplay where mark control and timing matter as much as point value.
Practice modes improve finishing, scoring consistency, and accuracy through repeatable drills.
Choose competition-oriented modes for ranking-style sessions and practice modes when you want structured training goals.

sections.heading: X01 (301 / 501 / 701)
paragraphs: X01 is the classic match format and works well as a default competitive format.
Players start at 301, 501, or 701 and subtract their turn score toward zero. The leg is won by hitting exactly zero with a finishing double or bull.
Choose X01 when you want a full-match feel with familiar scoring goals, clear wins by leg, and shared rules across sessions.
Suitable for beginners (learn checkout discipline), casual players (steady match rhythm), and competitive players (standard comparison play).
Compared with other modes, X01 is score-subtraction based and win-condition focused, rather than target-order or mark-closure driven.

sections.heading: Cricket
paragraphs: Cricket uses numbers 15 to 20 plus the bullseye. Each target needs three closures before you can score freely on it when the opponent has not also closed it.
Cricket is a good fit when you want tactical turns where one miss or hit at the right moment changes pressure across the whole leg.
Suitable for casual, experienced, and competitive players who want a strategy-first format and want to improve target control.
It differs from X01 and All 5s because success is measured by closing and managing targets, not by reaching a fixed total.
Use Cricket when you want a deeper decision loop between turns.

sections.heading: Snooker Darts
paragraphs: Snooker Darts adapts traditional snooker scoring: reds and colour values are represented by the dartboard sequence, and the goal is to score more than your opponent while clearing your required targets.
Choose Snooker Darts when you want a structured scoring approach with more planned shot selection than pure number racing.
Suitable for beginner-to-casual players who want variety, and experienced players who want deliberate play planning.
It differs from Cricket and One to 20 by blending sequence and colour-value priorities into the same run.

sections.heading: One to 20
paragraphs: One to 20 is a sequential target format. Players must hit numbers in order from 1 up to 20, with three darts per turn.
Choose One to 20 to build consistency and controlled repetition before moving into more open scoring formats.
This is beginner-friendly and works well for casual players who want a simple progression goal with steady improvement.
Unlike Cricket and X01, this mode is fixed-order play: you are rewarded for sequence discipline rather than scoreboard strategy.

sections.heading: Bob's 27
paragraphs: Bob's 27 starts each player at 27. You play through doubles from low to high in order, with misses carrying a clear penalty and hits advancing progress.
Choose this mode when you want pressure-specific double practice and elimination-style focus.
Suitable for intermediate to experienced players who want to train consistent double selection and high-stakes finishing decisions.
Bob's 27 differs from other modes by mixing ordered doubles with a direct penalty on rounds that miss the current target.

sections.heading: Hare & Hound
paragraphs: Hare & Hound is a two-side chase game: the Hare and Hound each begin on fixed positions and advance when their current target is hit.
Choose it for compact sessions with momentum shifts and a chase objective that feels different from score accumulation.
Good for casual-to-experienced players who want a light tactical format with clear end conditions.
It differs from all other modes by using positional progression and catch logic instead of score totals.

sections.heading: Halve It
paragraphs: In Halve It, each turn focuses on a single active target, and only hits on that target score for the round.
Choose Halve It when you want precision-focused practice on the same target pattern.
Suitable for beginner-to-casual and casual players building accuracy habits, while experienced players can use it to isolate weak shot zones.
Halve It differs from Bob's 27 and X01 because misses can halve your score, which puts strong emphasis on consistency rather than recovery.

sections.heading: All 5s
paragraphs: All 5s rewards turn totals that are divisible by five; those totals are converted into points, with the objective to reach exactly 51.
Choose this mode when your goal is short, repeatable accuracy practice with a clear scoring checkpoint.
Suitable for beginner through competitive players, and especially helpful as a warm-up before match-focused sessions.
All 5s differs from other modes by combining arithmetic timing and divisibility planning with strict bust handling.
